title: JavaScript Runtime, Tooling, and Evidence · Mastery Check
summary: Trace a small service-status script from source through parsing, execution, console evidence, and a user-visible result. Every item uses a fresh case and misconception-specific distractors. Planned artifact: Record independent decisions and explanations for a reproducible service-status runtime notebook. Learners progress through prediction, explicit theory, changed-case practice, causal debugging, and evidence defense.
objectives: Distinguish ECMAScript language behavior from browser, worker, and server host APIs.
Trace source parsing, module instantiation, execution contexts, jobs, and observable output.
Use console evidence, breakpoints, scope inspection, watch expressions, and stack traces without relying on logging alone.
Preserve previously learned constraints and attach observable changed-case evidence.

starterFiles.javascript:
// javascript-basics cumulative stakeholder program.
// Add one runnable behavior, assertion, changed case, and evidence return per activity.

steps.title: Diagnose the hidden failure
steps.instruction:
quiz case — Normalize service inputs [" 2 ", 2, null, {value:2}] without implicit-coercion surprises. Record typeof, equality, parsed value, and the exact branch that rejects the object case.

The visible result looks correct, but its invariant or value contract is unverified. What must happen next?
steps.why: The decision supplies observable evidence for record runtime, module mode, inputs, locale, time, and dependency conditions needed to reproduce behavior.

content.text: Normalize service inputs [" 2 ", 2, null, {value:2}] without implicit-coercion surprises. Record typeof, equality, parsed value, and the exact branch that rejects the object case.

content.title: Fault-isolation decision
content.text: Select evidence that identifies a cause, not a patch that only hides the Reproducible Environment symptom.

content.value: MDN Web API Reference — Controls DOM, events, workers, fetch, storage, abort, and browser security boundary coverage.

Use the documented misconception as a counterexample target: The same script behaves identically in every JavaScript host.

stimulus.title: Reproducible Environment failure evidence
stimulus.caption: A plausible visible result conflicts with the governing contract and requires a cause-level repair.

lines.label: observed
lines.text: The same script behaves identically in every JavaScript host.

lines.label: required
lines.text: A changed authentic task independently demonstrates this outcome: Record runtime, module mode, inputs, locale, time, and dependency conditions needed to reproduce behavior.

options.text: Apply the visible technique immediately, omit assumptions, and treat one successful output as complete proof.
Run the boundary case, inspect the actual mechanism, then accept or repair the claim.
Accept the result because visible output is the highest authority.

steps.title: Reconstruct the defensible process
steps.instruction: Order the Runtime Host evidence cycle for this quiz task before implementing it.
steps.why: Correct order keeps Runtime Host assumptions visible before execution and verifies its result against a boundary case afterward.

options.text: Declare inputs, observable output, ownership, and the runtime behavior being tested
Trace values, bindings, receivers, mutations, and queued work before editing code
Implement the smallest behavior and retain an assertion over the actual result
Run boundary and changed-event cases, inspect failures, and remove accidental coupling

steps.title: Defend one difficult decision
steps.instruction: Write the quiz evidence note for Reproducible Environment: explain the governing condition, one failure, and how this concrete case was verified — Normalize service inputs [" 5 ", 5, null, {value:5}] without implicit-coercion surprises. Record typeof, equality, parsed value, and the exact branch that rejects the object case.
steps.why: Retrieval and explanation expose gaps that recognition questions can hide.

content.title: Evidence defense
content.text: Use the case, one assumption, one observable result, and one limitation. Do not copy the competency statement: Record runtime, module mode, inputs, locale, time, and dependency conditions needed to reproduce behavior.
